ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a rose-specific cordial code; A0FJP (soft drinks, diluted from syrup) is the closest defensible category anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is one glass of approximately 250 ml, delivering around 107.5 kcal and 27 g of sugar from the diluted rose syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Rose Drink (from Syrup) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Rose Drink (from Syrup), cr_id D03050003. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this primary regional source.

gi_source.title: Sucrose and sugar-sweetened beverages GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008). International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for rose drink from syrup in the University of Sydney GI Database. Rose syrup drink is essentially a sucrose-based sugar-sweetened beverage. GI of 65 is assigned by analogy to sucrose solution / sugar-sweetened cordial drinks as documented in Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 27.0) / 100 = 17.55, rounded to 17.6. Serving = 250 g × 10.8 g carbohydrate/100 g = 27 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.8 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention and weight gain
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid rose drink from syrup; the 10.8 g sugar per 100 ml exceeds the AHA 2018 beverage sugar threshold of 5 g/100 ml and promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and adiposity that independently raise blood pressure, regardless of the low sodium content of 3 mg/100 ml.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: 10.8 g rapidly absorbed added sugar per 100 ml with zero dietary fibre causes acute postprandial blood glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid rose drink from syrup entirely; a standard 250 ml glass delivers approximately 27 g of rapidly absorbed added sugar with no fibre buffer, causing acute postprandial glycaemic spikes contraindicated per ADA 2023 Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk via dyslipidaemia, weight gain, and systemic inflammation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid rose drink from syrup; regular intake of sugar-sweetened beverages at this sugar concentration (10.8 g/100 ml) is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased LDL-cholesterol, and systemic inflammation per AHA 2021 Dietary Guidance to Improve Cardiovascular Health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: 10.8 g added sugar per 100 ml with no anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid rose drink from syrup; the high refined sugar content (10.8 g/100 ml) with zero fibre, omega-3, polyphenols, or anti-inflammatory micronutrients promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity and provides no therapeutic benefit for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rose drink from syrup is a popular sweet floral beverage widely consumed in Singapore and across South and Southeast Asia. It is prepared by diluting commercially produced rose syrup — a concentrated sugar syrup flavoured with rose essence or rose water and typically coloured pink or red — with plain water, usually in a ratio of approximately 1 part syrup to 4–5 parts water. The resulting drink is bright pink, intensely sweet, and carries a distinctive floral rose aroma. It is served chilled or over ice and is a common offering at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and festive occasions. Nutritionally, rose drink from syrup is a sugar-sweetened beverage providing 43 kcal per 100 ml, with all calories derived from carbohydrates (10.8 g/100 ml), entirely as sugar. It contains no protein, no fat, no dietary fibre, and negligible sodium (3 mg/100 ml). A standard 250 ml glass delivers approximately 27 g of added sugar, exceeding the WHO daily free sugar recommendation of 25 g in a single serving. Clinically, it is contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid glycaemic impact, for those with hypertension due to insulin-driven sodium retention from high sugar load, and for cardiovascular disease patients due to its association with dyslipidaemia and systemic inflammation. It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis management. Healthier alternatives include unsweetened rose water in sparkling water, herbal teas, or plain water.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in rose syrup drink?
Can diabetics drink rose syrup drink?
Is bandung the same as rose drink?
Is rose drink suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03050003), which is the primary approved regional source for Singaporean food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches. GI is estimated by analogy to sucrose-based sugar-sweetened beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), which is the closest defensible approved GI source.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero as rose drink from syrup is a sugar-water beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients. fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation — rose syrup contains only sugar, water, rose flavouring, and colouring agents with no fat-bearing components. saturated_fat_g and dietary_fibre_g are confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this diluted rose syrup beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. GI note: GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose solution and sugar-sweetened cordial reference values from Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for rose drink from syrup was found in any approved source. Ontology note: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for rose drink from syrup in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2026.
